entry.title: CVE-2026-42897, CVE-2026-45657, and AI-Enabled Phishing Drive June Threat Coverage
entry.content:
This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with AI-generated sites, adversary-in-the-middle kits, event-themed fraud, and low-content email payloads complicating detection. Microsoft patched CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited high-severity spoofing vulnerability in Exchange Server affecting Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition. The flaw can be exploited remotely without privileges by sending a specially crafted email that, under certain Outlook Web Access interaction conditions, executes arbitrary JavaScript in the user’s browser context. CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 15 and set a May 29 remediation deadline for U.S. federal agencies. Microsoft’s June Patch Tuesday fixed more than 200 security flaws, described by multiple vulnerability trackers as the largest monthly release in the program’s history. Among the most severe was CVE-2026-45657, a critical Windows kernel vulnerability rated 9.8 that could allow remote full system compromise without user interaction and was characterized by ZDI as wormable. The release also included CVE-2026-41091, an actively exploited Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege flaw that could allow an attacker with an existing foothold to gain full control of a system. The same Microsoft coverage included three disclosed zero-days, including CVE-2026-50507, a BitLocker bypass tied to the pseudonymous researcher Nightmare Eclipse. Separately, Microsoft reported that a small percentage of devices upgraded to Windows 11 versions 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the latest cumulative update, with errors including 0x80073712 and 0x800f0993 and logs referencing component store corruption or missing hydration candidates. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported that phishing activity declined by roughly 20% year over year in both 2024 and 2025, while attackers shifted toward more targeted and personalized lures resembling routine business workflows. The report identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances, with 37,447, or 9.06%, flagged as malicious, and attributed builders including Manus AI, Blackbox AI, and Anything AI. ThreatLabz also found that 95.2% of phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels. The same research described phishing kits using adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle techniques to capture credentials and MFA codes during live login flows, enabling session-level compromise rather than simple password theft. ThreatLabz also recorded 89.9 million hostile interactions with external decoys over six months and observed more than 121,000 distinct AWS-hosted IPs probing customer environments, underscoring the scale of opportunistic reconnaissance against exposed infrastructure. Event-themed fraud is already appearing around the FIFA World Cup 2026. The FBI warned that threat actors are creating fraudulent FIFA-affiliated websites to collect personal data, conduct financial fraud, and sell fake goods or services, while Cyble confirmed that many FBI-flagged domains remained active at publication time. Reported examples included ww-fifa[.]com, a fake hospitality and ticket portal requesting personal and payment details, and fifaworldcup-careers[.]com, a fake recruitment domain associated with multiple IP addresses and wildcard SSL certificates. IRONSCALES analyzed an “ACH payment” email sent from carlaburbo@keystonenatural[.]com to an accounts receivable team, with ap@keystonenatural[.]com copied. The message passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication at final delivery, while original authentication results showed dkim=none and dmarc=none, and ARC validation at i=2 returned cv=fail, indicating a broken forwarding chain of trust. The email’s payload consisted entirely of a single PNG attachment named image002.png, with no message body, no links, and no OCR-extracted text. Themis flagged the message at 54% confidence as credential theft, and one mailbox was quarantined, illustrating how image-only payloads and authentication-chain inconsistencies can coexist with apparently successful final-delivery authentication outcomes. A related control-validation theme appeared in reporting on automated pentesting limitations. The article argued that clean automated pentest results can create false confidence because they show exploitable paths but not whether SIEM rules fired, EDR alerts were raised, or SOC analysts had sufficient signal to act. It contrasted automated pentesting with breach and attack simulation, framing the latter as focused on whether controls blocked, detected, logged, or missed known malicious behaviors.

Microsoft patches Exchange Server zero-day exploited in attacks — Must Read

Microsoft released Exchange Server security updates for CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited zero-day that can execute arbitrary JavaScript against Outlook Web Access users via crafted emails. Admins should patch immediately and keep Microsoft’s temporary mitigation enabled for added protection.

  • Microsoft patched CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited high-severity spoofing vulnerability in Exchange Server that enables cross-site scripting attacks against Outlook Web Access users.
  • CVE-2026-42897 affects Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition, and it can be exploited remotely by attackers with no privileges.
  • Microsoft said an attacker could exploit the issue by sending a specially crafted email that, if opened in Outlook Web Access under certain interaction conditions, can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser context.
  • Microsoft advised administrators to install the June 2026 Security Updates for their Exchange Server version as soon as possible and to keep the temporary mitigation in place for additional protection.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-42897 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 15 and ordered U.S. government agencies to patch affected servers by May 29.

Microsoft ships largest Patch Tuesday on record, with one bug under active attack — Must Read

Microsoft released its largest Patch Tuesday ever, fixing more than 200 flaws as AI-assisted vulnerability discovery drives patch volumes higher. Organizations should prioritize updates, especially for an actively exploited Microsoft Defender privilege-escalation bug and a critical wormable Windows kernel flaw.

  • Microsoft released fixes for more than 200 security flaws in its June Patch Tuesday, which multiple trackers described as the largest monthly release in the program's history.
  • Microsoft’s security leadership said AI tools are driving a surge in vulnerability discovery, and the company expects Patch Tuesday releases to continue trending larger.
  • CVE-2026-45657 is a critical Windows kernel flaw rated 9.8 that could allow a remote attacker to take full control of a machine with no user action and was described by ZDI as wormable.
  • CVE-2026-41091 is an actively exploited Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege flaw that could let an attacker with an existing foothold gain full control of a system.
  • Three zero-day flaws were disclosed, including a BitLocker bypass tracked as CVE-2026-50507, which is tied to the pseudonymous researcher Nightmare Eclipse.

One Click to Compromise: ThreatLabz 2026 Phishing and Initial Access Report — Should Read

Zscaler ThreatLabz reports that phishing is becoming less about mass volume and more about targeted, AI-assisted campaigns that use encrypted traffic, realistic fake sites, and real-time session theft to bypass MFA. The main takeaway is that organizations need to reduce exposed entry points and inspect encrypted web activity because a single convincing click can now quickly become initial access.

  • Zscaler ThreatLabz observed phishing activity decline by about 20% year over year in both 2024 and 2025, while attackers shifted toward targeted, personalized lures that resemble routine work.
  • ThreatLabz identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances and flagged 37,447 of them, or 9.06%, as malicious, with attributed builders including Manus AI, Blackbox AI, and Anything AI.
  • ThreatLabz found that 95.2% of phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels, making TLS/SSL inspection important for detecting credential theft, session abuse, and malicious redirects.
  • ThreatLabz observed phishing kits using adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle techniques to capture credentials and MFA codes during active login flows, enabling session-level compromise.
  • ThreatLabz recorded 89.9 million hostile interactions with external decoys in six months and logged more than 121,000 distinct AWS-hosted IPs probing customer environments.

FIFA World Cup 2026 Scams Are Already Active: Fake Domains, Phishing Sites, and How to Stay Safe — Should Read

FBI and Cyble warn that cybercriminals are already using fake FIFA World Cup 2026 domains to run ticket, hospitality, streaming, and job scams that steal personal and financial data. Fans and job seekers should only use the official fifa.com site, avoid sponsored or unsolicited links, and report suspected scams to IC3.

  • The FBI warned that threat actors are creating fraudulent versions of FIFA-affiliated websites to steal personal information, conduct financial fraud, and sell fake products and services.
  • Cyble researchers analyzed FBI-flagged FIFA-related domains and confirmed that many remained active and operational at the time of publication.
  • The scams use typosquatting domains to impersonate FIFA sites for fake ticket sales, hospitality packages, job listings, and other fraud schemes.
  • Cyble identified ww-fifa[.]com as a fake FIFA World Cup 2026 hospitality and ticket portal that requests personal and financial information through fraudulent purchase prompts.
  • Cyble researchers found that the fake recruitment domain fifaworldcup-careers[.]com was flagged by security vendors and used infrastructure including multiple IP addresses and wildcard SSL certificates.

Microsoft: Some Windows PCs fail to install latest monthly updates — Should Read

Microsoft says some PCs upgraded to Windows 11 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the June 2026 cumulative updates with errors 0x80073712 or 0x800f0993. A restart should fix many unmanaged and Home devices, but already-affected systems may need a DISM package removal or an in-place upgrade.

  • Microsoft warned that a small percentage of devices upgraded from Windows 10 versions 21H2 or 22H2, or Windows 11 version 23H2, to Windows 11 versions 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the latest cumulative update.
  • Affected devices may show Windows Update errors 0x80073712 or 0x800f0993, and Windows Update logs may show PSFX_E_REBASE_HYDRATION_CANDIDATES_MISSING or ERROR_SXS_COMPONENT_STORE_CORRUPT.
  • Microsoft said a fix will roll out to unmanaged enterprise devices and personal Home edition PCs after a system restart, with no new devices in those categories expected to be affected after May 19, 2026, 6:30 p.m. PT.
  • Microsoft released June 2026 Patch Tuesday updates intended to install automatically during upgrades to Windows 11 and prevent the issue from occurring on other affected devices.
  • For systems already upgraded to Windows 11 version 24H2 or 25H2 where the issue is not addressed, Microsoft advised removing the affected package with a DISM command or performing a Windows 11 in-place upgrade if needed.

The Invoice Email With No Text to Scan: Image-Only Payload From a Compromised Account With a Broken ARC Chain — May Read

A compromised vendor email account sent a fully authenticated “ACH payment” message to an accounts receivable team with the entire payload hidden in a PNG image, bypassing text, link, and sandbox scanning. The key warning signs were first-time sender behavior, failed ARC provenance, and missing original DKIM/DMARC despite final-hop authentication passing.

  • An email with the subject “ACH payment” was sent from carlaburbo@keystonenatural[.]com to the accounts receivable team of a global flavoring company and CC’d ap@keystonenatural[.]com.
  • The email passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication at final delivery, but Authentication-Results-Original showed dkim=none and dmarc=none.
  • ARC validation at i=2 returned cv=fail, indicating that the forwarding chain of trust was broken.
  • The entire message payload was a single PNG attachment named image002.png that was 63,801 bytes, with no body text, no links, and no OCR text extracted.
  • Themis flagged the message at 54% confidence as credential theft, and one mailbox was quarantined.

Your Automated Pentest Looks Clean. See What It Missed in This Expert Webinar — Optional

A clean automated pentest report can create false confidence because it may only show that attack paths were tested, not whether security controls detected or blocked them. The webinar argues teams should pair automated pentesting with broader control validation to prioritize risks based on what defenders would actually catch.

  • The article argues that a clean or stable automated pentest report can create false confidence because the tool may have reached the limits of what it can see.
  • Picus Security frames validation across six surfaces and places automated pentesting on the attack-path surface, leaving areas such as detection rules, cloud configurations, identity controls, and AI guardrails unproven.
  • The article states that when an automated pentesting tool exploits a technique, it does not show whether a SIEM rule fired, an EDR raised an alert, or a SOC had enough signal to act.
  • Breach and attack simulation asks whether a control blocked, detected, logged, or missed a known behavior, while automated pentesting asks how far an attacker could move through an exploitable path.
  • The webinar focuses on helping teams turn findings into a ranked queue based on whether security controls actually caught the behavior.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with...

entry.title: Microsoft YellowKey, phpBB RCE, ServiceNow Exploitation, and Ivanti Sentry Root RCE
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that enable full system or administrative compromise. A parallel theme is the increasing role of advanced AI systems in vulnerability discovery and the controls vendors are placing around cyber-capable models. Microsoft’s June 2026 Patch Tuesday addressed three zero-day vulnerabilities known as GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma, and YellowKey. GreenPlasma and MiniPlasma are local privilege escalation flaws in the Collaborative Translation Framework and Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver, respectively, and can allow attackers to obtain SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows systems prior to remediation. YellowKey is a Windows Recovery Environment backdoor affecting unpatched Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022/2025 systems, enabling attackers with physical access to bypass BitLocker protections. The vulnerabilities were disclosed by a researcher using the handle Nightmare Eclipse, who framed the release as a protest against Microsoft Security Response Center disclosure practices. The same researcher has reportedly released proof-of-concept exploits for additional Windows zero-days, including BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend, and RoguePlanet. This creates a broader exposure pattern in which defenders face not only patched vulnerabilities, but also an expanding set of public exploit artifacts tied to unresolved or recently disclosed Windows flaws. Aikido reported a critical phpBB authentication bypass vulnerability that can lead to remote code execution and complete takeover of the underlying system. The flaw affects phpBB 3.3.16 and below, as well as 4.0.0-a2, and is exploitable in the default configuration with a single unauthenticated HTTP request to obtain a valid session as any user. phpBB released version 3.3.17 after the issue was reported through HackerOne on June 2, with a working patch published on June 6. The update also introduces a breaking change for OAuth authentication users because the redirect URI handler has moved to /user/oauth/authenticate/..., which may affect deployments relying on existing OAuth flows. Ivanti also disclosed two critical Sentry secure mobile gateway vulnerabilities. CVE-2026-10520 is a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw allowing remote code execution as root, while CVE-2026-10523 is an authentication bypass that can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to create rogue administrative accounts and gain full administrative access; Ivanti stated it had no evidence of exploitation at disclosure time. ServiceNow reported that unknown threat actors exploited a security issue to gain greater unauthorized access to susceptible customer instances than intended. The issue does not currently have a CVE identifier, and details first emerged publicly through Reddit before ServiceNow described anomalous activity and successful queries of instance tables affecting a subset of customers. ServiceNow applied a hosted-instance security update on June 5, changing an endpoint configuration to restrict access to authenticated users. The affected population includes customers on the Australia platform release and customers that made certain configuration changes to instances running releases prior to Australia. The incident is notable because it involves exploitation against a major enterprise workflow platform and successful access to customer instance data. ServiceNow said affected customers were notified after evidence of successful table queries was observed. Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 publicly while limiting its cyber-unrestricted counterpart, Claude Mythos 5, to vetted cyber defenders and critical infrastructure operators. The company said Mythos-class models can find and exploit software vulnerabilities at a level where unrestricted public access would provide attackers with significant uplift. Claude Fable 5 uses safety classifiers that route flagged cyber, biology, chemistry, and distillation requests to the weaker Claude Opus 4.8 rather than refusing them outright. Anthropic also said that during Project Glasswing, it and roughly 50 partners used Claude Mythos Preview to identify more than ten thousand high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software. Anthropic will require 30-day data retention for traffic on Fable 5, Mythos 5, and future models at this capability level, while stating that retained data will not be used for training or non-safety purposes. The release reflects a widening gap between public AI capabilities and restricted cyber-specialized systems intended for controlled defensive use.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that...

entry.title: Proto6 protobuf.js RCE Flaws and AI Security Tooling Developments
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by two themes: newly disclosed Node.js ecosystem risk in protobuf.js, and continued maturation of AI tooling for security operations and research. The most immediately operationally relevant item is the Proto6 vulnerability set, which affects protobuf.js consumers across application, cloud library, messaging, and CI/CD contexts. Researchers at Cyera disclosed six vulnerabilities collectively named Proto6 in protobuf.js, a widely used JavaScript implementation for Protocol Buffers. The issues can expose affected Node.js applications to remote code execution and denial-of-service conditions, particularly where applications deserialize Protobuf data or generate code from schemas. The flaws center on protobuf.js treating schema and metadata as trusted by default. According to the reporting, this behavior can allow attacker-controlled schema-related input to influence application behavior and, in certain paths, reach code execution conditions. The most severe issue highlighted is CVE-2026-44291, which can allow arbitrary JavaScript execution when attacker-controlled input reaches a prototype pollution gadget before protobuf.js encodes or decodes a message. The affected exposure surface extends beyond direct protobuf.js use. The article identifies Google Cloud client libraries, messaging frameworks such as Baileys, Node.js services handling Protobuf data, and CI/CD pipelines that generate code from schemas as potentially impacted environments. Fixes are reported in protobufjs 7.5.6 and 8.0.2, along with protobufjs-cli 1.2.1 and 2.0.2. Several articles in this window focused on AI-enabled cybersecurity tooling and model governance rather than active intrusion activity. SpecterOps introduced GhostWorks, an AI-focused engineering and research initiative intended to test frontier AI capabilities against identity-security problems, document limitations, and transition successful prototypes beyond the lab. The initiative follows earlier SpecterOps AI work, including OpenAI adversarial red teaming and participation in OpenAI’s Trusted Access for Cyber program. SpecterOps also published a technical examination of GEPA, an optimization framework for refining prompts using LLM inference instead of reinforcement learning. The proof-of-concept optimized an AGENTS.md prompt for a web CTF agent with shell execution, file access, web request, and flag submission tooling. Its evaluation function rewarded solved challenges and weighted turn efficiency more heavily than runtime, using agent run artifacts such as reasoning output, tool use, return values, and errors to identify patterns. The GEPA demonstration produced mixed but informative results: one challenge score decreased slightly while two improved, with signs of overfitting attributed to the small training and validation set. The article positions GEPA as measurable prompt engineering for security agents, especially where practitioners use closed-source or remote-hosted models and have limited compute resources. Separately, Anthropic began rolling out Claude Fable 5, described as a safer version of a Mythos-class model with guardrails for sensitive domains including offensive cybersecurity, biology, and chemistry. The unrestricted Claude Mythos 5 model is reportedly limited to highly vetted trusted partners, including government cyberdefenders and selected life sciences researchers. BleepingComputer reported high usage consumption during testing, including approximately 1 million tokens in 8 minutes and rapid exhaustion of a $100 Max subscription daily allowance when using Workflow mode and high model thinking.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by two themes: newly disclosed Node.js ecosystem risk in protobuf.js, and continued maturation of AI tooling for security operations and research. The most immediately operationally relevant item is the Proto6 vulnerability set, which affects protobuf.js consumers...

entry.title: Ivanti Sentry CVE-2026-10520 RCE and Microsoft Defender RoguePlanet Zero-Day
entry.content: This digest centers on two high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise security technologies: Ivanti Sentry and Microsoft Defender. The reporting highlights a pre-authentication root-level remote code execution path in Ivanti Sentry and a publicly released Microsoft Defender local privilege escalation exploit capable of spawning SYSTEM shells on fully patched Windows systems. WatchTowr Labs published technical analysis of CVE-2026-10520, an OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry versions before R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1. According to the analysis, the vulnerable unauthenticated endpoint is /mics/api/v2/sentry/mics-config/handleMessage, where a user-controlled message parameter is passed into backend configuration handling logic. The issue can allow a remote unauthenticated attacker to achieve root-level remote code execution. The researchers described how the message value is parsed as a MICS configuration command into fields including command, module, XPath, and XML body. They found that the EXECUTE path could reach executeNativeCommand through reflection-based backend handling, and reproduced the vulnerability by sending a POST request containing a commandexec XML block with uname -a in the reqandres field. The HTTP response returned command output, demonstrating practical exploitation of the flaw. The patched Ivanti implementation reportedly hardcodes the command passed to handleMessage and adds Apache configuration rules that block access to the vulnerable endpoint, redirecting unauthenticated requests to the login page. The analysis is notable because it provides endpoint-level and execution-path detail for a vulnerability that sits on an exposed management/security product boundary, where unauthenticated reachability materially increases operational risk. BleepingComputer reported on RoguePlanet, a Microsoft Defender zero-day exploit released by researcher Nightmare Eclipse. The exploit is described as a race condition that can spawn a command prompt with SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 devices, though its reliability varies by system. ThreatLocker told BleepingComputer it reproduced the exploit on fully patched Windows 11 systems with KB5094126 installed and confirmed it worked as described. Nightmare Eclipse said RoguePlanet was originally developed as a remote code execution vulnerability involving Microsoft Defender’s handling of files on remote SMB shares. The researcher claimed Microsoft silently hardened Defender in mid-May to block junction attacks, changing the exploit’s practical impact from the originally described RCE scenario to a local privilege escalation path. The release also forms part of an ongoing public dispute between Nightmare Eclipse and Microsoft over vulnerability disclosure and bug bounty practices, following previous releases named BlueHammer, RedSun, GreenPlasma, and YellowKey. Together, these disclosures underscore continued scrutiny of defensive and mobile infrastructure products as high-value exploit targets. One case involves unauthenticated command execution in an enterprise gateway product, while the other concerns privilege escalation in a default Windows security component, creating distinct but operationally significant exposure patterns for defenders tracking exploit development and public proof-of-concept activity.
entry.summary: This digest centers on two high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise security technologies: Ivanti Sentry and Microsoft Defender. The reporting highlights a pre-authentication root-level remote code execution path in Ivanti Sentry and a publicly released Microsoft...

entry.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, Veeam CVE-2026-44963, and Miasma Supply-Chain Fallout
entry.content: During this coverage window, reporting was dominated by large-scale vulnerability remediation, software supply-chain compromise, and the accelerating role of AI in vulnerability discovery. Microsoft addressed 200 flaws including three publicly disclosed zero-days, while Veeam patched a critical Backup & Replication RCE issue affecting domain-joined servers. Separate reporting detailed fallout from the Miasma/Shai-Hulud ecosystem and new malware and authentication research relevant to defenders. Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday fixed 200 vulnerabilities, including 33 critical issues and three publicly disclosed zero-days: CVE-2026-45586 in Windows Collaborative Translation Framework, CVE-2026-49160 in HTTP.sys, and CVE-2026-50507 in Windows BitLocker. Microsoft said none of the three zero-days were known to have been exploited. The release also introduced a MaxHeadersCount registry setting tied to mitigation of the HTTP/2 Bomb denial-of-service issue affecting HTTP.sys. Microsoft also released mandatory Windows 11 cumulative updates KB5094126 and KB5093998 for supported 25H2/24H2 and 23H2 systems. The updates include security fixes, reliability improvements, and feature changes such as Shared Audio enhancements, Task Manager NPU visibility, Multi-App Camera policy controls, and setup-time custom user folder naming. Microsoft reported no new known issues for this Patch Tuesday release. Veeam released fixes for CVE-2026-44963, a critical Backup & Replication vulnerability that can allow remote code execution on domain-joined backup servers. The issue affects version 12.3.2.4465 and earlier version 12 builds and is fixed in 12.3.2.4854, while version 13.x is not affected due to architectural changes. Veeam said exploitation requires any low-privileged authenticated domain user and noted there were no reports of active exploitation. Microsoft confirmed that some GitHub repositories were temporarily removed after a security incident involving 73 open-source projects compromised to inject an information stealer. Some repositories were restored after review, while others remained offline as the investigation continued. Microsoft said a small number of customers who may have pulled affected content were notified through support channels. Earlier reporting said the removed repositories spanned Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations and briefly disrupted CI workflows, including Azure/functions-action usage. Researchers linked the incident to broader Miasma/Shai-Hulud supply-chain activity involving Microsoft GitHub resources, Red Hat npm packages, PyPI packages, and durabletask. Analysis of the Miasma payload found it could trigger automatic code execution when an affected repository was opened in an AI-powered coding tool or IDE. StepSecurity introduced a Threat Center API endpoint that returns compromised open source components associated with a specific incident. The endpoint provides ecosystem, component, affected version, severity, verification status, description, and threat intelligence identifiers. The company described use cases including SIEM enrichment, SOC workflow automation, and correlation against SBOMs or lockfiles. XBOW reported testing Anthropic's Mythos Preview across offensive security benchmarks, workflows, integrations, and API-based agents. The testing found the model stronger than prior systems at identifying vulnerability candidates, particularly with source code access, while live-site interaction remained important for exploitability validation. XBOW also reported strong results in native-code vulnerability discovery, reverse engineering, and browser-based visual workflows, with mixed judgment and high estimated cost. Mitiga highlighted Anthropic reporting that Project Glasswing partners used Mythos Preview to find more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software. The article argued that the bottleneck has shifted from discovery to verification, disclosure, and patching, citing 75 patched issues out of 530 disclosed high- or critical-severity bugs at the time of writing. It also framed modern exposure around pre-patch exploitation timelines and very short eCrime breakout times. Health-ISAC discussed President Trump's AI executive order through a healthcare cybersecurity lens. Errol Weiss said the sector's opportunity lies in faster coordination, especially as attackers use AI to compress the time from vulnerability discovery to exploitation. The article emphasized timely, actionable information sharing through ISAC channels and measurable resilience improvements for critical infrastructure. Zscaler ThreatLabz analyzed MLTBackdoor, a new malware family identified in May 2026 and assessed as likely connected to a ransomware-related actor. The malware was delivered through a multi-stage ClickFix chain using endpointdlp.dll and an RC4-encrypted data.bin payload. It uses heavy obfuscation, API hashing, indirect system calls, anti-analysis checks, filesystem commands, a Beacon Object File loader, and encrypted TLS-based command-and-control over port 443. SpecterOps published research on Kerberos user-to-user authentication in Windows environments. The article explains how U2U encrypts a service ticket with a target user's TGT session key, including the KERB-TGT-REQUEST and ENC-TKT-IN-SKEY flow. It also details RDP Network Level Authentication usage during CredSSP negotiation and how UnPAC-the-Hash combines PKINIT and U2U to recover a user's NT hash from PACCREDENTIALINFO.
entry.summary: During this coverage window, reporting was dominated by large-scale vulnerability remediation, software supply-chain compromise, and the accelerating role of AI in vulnerability discovery. Microsoft addressed 200 flaws including three publicly disclosed zero-days, while Veeam patched a critical...

entry.title: CVE-2026-11645, CVE-2025-8088, SiribClone, Tchap Breach Drive Threat Digest
entry.content: This digest highlights active exploitation, espionage targeting military personnel, account hijacking, and abuse of trusted identity and collaboration workflows. Several reports show attackers relying less on novel malware alone and more on legitimate platforms, OAuth flows, cloud services, residential proxy infrastructure, and social engineering to gain access or evade detection. A previously undocumented espionage group tracked as SiribClone has targeted Russian military personnel since at least summer 2025, particularly in border regions and combat zones. The group posed as women seeking romantic relationships or as humanitarian volunteers to persuade servicemen to install malicious Android applications or enter Telegram credentials into spoofed sites. SiribClone’s Android spyware, SafeLoveStealer, collected files, location data, and other information from infected devices, and could remotely activate microphones to record conversations. F6 researchers also identified an internal platform called Kontur that stored stolen Telegram sessions and included notes referencing ranks, unit designations, locations, and operational status. Separately, Trend Micro attributed exploitation of the WinRAR path traversal flaw CVE-2025-8088 against Ukrainian organizations to two Russia-aligned clusters: Earth Dahu, also known as Gamaredon, and SHADOW-EARTH-066, also known as UAC-0226. SHADOW-EARTH-066 used crafted RAR files with hidden NTFS Alternate Data Streams to deploy the GIFTEDCROOK stealer, while Earth Dahu used an HTA-to-VBScript chain delivering GammaPhish, GammaLoad, and GammaSteel components. AppOmni detailed how device code phishing abuses the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant to obtain valid access and refresh tokens without stealing passwords. The technique has expanded beyond Microsoft 365 to SaaS platforms including Salesforce and GitHub, and can bypass expectations around MFA, FIDO2, and passkeys because victims authenticate through legitimate provider workflows. Varonis Threat Labs tested an OpenClaw AI inbox agent named Pinchy and found that, in two simulated scenarios, the agent forwarded sensitive material such as AWS IAM keys, database passwords, SSH credentials, and a CRM export to external requesters. The research framed this as agent phishing, where plausible requests exploit an agent’s delegated trust, rather than indirect prompt injection through malicious embedded instructions. IRONSCALES reported a Google Forms editor invite sent to a multinational law firm through Google infrastructure that passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication checks. The sender display name matched the recipient’s name, while the Hebrew-language form referenced a property tax rights survey and used editor invite links that did not require Google sign-in. DINUM disclosed that France’s government encrypted messaging platform Tchap was breached through a compromised user account. ANSSI detected the incident, DINUM blocked the malicious account, and the French data protection authority CNIL was notified because personal data in accessible conversations may have been exposed. A threat actor claimed responsibility for the Tchap incident and alleged social engineering was used to access an education shard and scrape messages, files, and account information. DINUM also noted that Tchap public rooms can be joined by any user and are not encrypted, distinguishing them from protected private communications. Infoblox reported broad enterprise exposure to residential proxy infrastructure, finding that more than 65% of its Threat Defense Cloud customers made DNS queries in 2026 to domains used to access or orchestrate such networks. Monthly DNS queries to residential proxy domains grew from nearly 400 billion in January 2025 to more than 500 billion by April 2026, with Brightdata the most frequently observed service across customer environments. Google released Chrome updates addressing 74 vulnerabilities, including the actively exploited V8 zero-day CVE-2026-11645. The high-severity out-of-bounds memory access flaw could allow remote code execution inside the Chrome sandbox via a crafted HTML page in versions prior to 149.0.7827.103. University of Toronto researchers demonstrated a proof-of-concept self-replicating AI worm using a locally hosted open-weight large language model. In isolated tests on a deliberately vulnerable 33-host network, the worm autonomously identified vulnerabilities, gained elevated access, replicated across hosts, and in some cases rewrote its own code to bypass local security controls. A separate analysis of modern network security operations described risk arising from fragmented work between tools rather than from a simple lack of detections. The article characterized manual coordination across SIEM, firewalls, IAM, ITSM, cloud, monitoring, and messaging systems as a source of delays, drift, audit gaps, overprivileged access, and inconsistent enforcement.
entry.summary: This digest highlights active exploitation, espionage targeting military personnel, account hijacking, and abuse of trusted identity and collaboration workflows. Several reports show attackers relying less on novel malware alone and more on legitimate platforms, OAuth flows, cloud services,...

entry.title: CVE-2026-50751, CVE-2026-11645, and CVE-2026-42271 Exploited in the Wild
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of newly disclosed or recently patched vulnerabilities across remote access infrastructure, browser software, and AI application tooling. The highest-impact reporting centers on bugs that can enable unauthorized access, code execution, or post-compromise activity, with CISA adding urgency through federal remediation deadlines and Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog entries. A common theme is adversary focus on exposed access paths: VPN gateways, browser attack surface, and developer-facing AI proxy services. The reported exploitation includes ransomware-linked post-compromise activity, Chrome zero-day use in the wild, and a LiteLLM flaw that can be chained into unauthenticated remote code execution under certain conditions. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure affected Check Point devices against CVE-2026-50751 by June 11 after the flaw was exploited as a zero-day. The vulnerability allows unauthenticated remote attackers to bypass authentication and establish a remote access VPN connection on targeted Check Point Mobile Access/SSL VPNs, Remote Access VPNs, or Spark firewalls. The exposure is limited to specific legacy or weakened configurations, including deployments using the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol, gateways that do not require a machine certificate, and legacy Remote Access clients. Check Point reported exploitation beginning on May 7, with activity surging over the weekend and affecting a few dozen targeted organizations globally. The company also linked at least one post-compromise incident involving CVE-2026-50751 to a Qilin ransomware affiliate, elevating the issue from access abuse to ransomware-enabled intrusion risk. CISA also added CVE-2026-42271 in BerriAI LiteLLM to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog based on evidence of active exploitation. The flaw is a high-severity command injection issue affecting LiteLLM Python package versions 1.74.2 through versions earlier than 1.83.7, allowing any authenticated user to execute arbitrary commands on the host. Horizon3.ai reported that CVE-2026-42271 can be chained with CVE-2026-48710, a Starlette host header validation bypass, to achieve unauthenticated remote code execution against vulnerable LiteLLM deployments. BerriAI addressed the issue in LiteLLM 1.83.7 by requiring the PROXYADMIN role for two MCP server preview test endpoints, while the reported chain highlights how AI infrastructure components can become externally exploitable when combined with framework-level weaknesses. Google released emergency Chrome updates for CVE-2026-11645, a high-severity zero-day vulnerability in the V8 JavaScript engine that the company said has been exploited in the wild. The patched Stable Desktop versions are 149.0.7827.102 for Windows and Linux and 149.0.7827.103 for Mac. CVE-2026-11645 is described as an out-of-bounds read and write weakness exploitable through crafted HTML pages. Successful exploitation can allow remote attackers to execute arbitrary code inside Chrome’s sandbox, access data beyond the intended memory buffer through heap corruption, expose sensitive information, or trigger browser crashes. Google said access to bug details and related links may remain restricted until a majority of users have received the fix, consistent with its handling of actively exploited browser vulnerabilities.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of newly disclosed or recently patched vulnerabilities across remote access infrastructure, browser software, and AI application tooling. The highest-impact reporting centers on bugs that can enable unauthorized access, code execution, or...
